text: The results of tests of austenitic stainless steels over the temperature range of 700 to $900^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ show that their properties are essentially equivalent in argon and in hydrogen environments.

The results of a series of tests at $815^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ in environments containing various partial pressures of oxygen in argon are presented which show that the properties of stainless steels can be markedly altered by the presence of a few parts per million of oxygen.
Several gaseous coolants being used in reactors already in operation or under construction include helium, carbon dioxide, air, and $\mathrm{N}_2$ -0.5 vol $\%$ $\mathrm{O}_2$ .
Except for its hazardous nature, the properties of hydrogen [1] establish it as the superior gaseous coolant.

The classes of materials studied were austenitic stainless steels, nickel-base alloys, and refractory metals, and the data deal primarily with a few representative materials from each class: type 304 stainless steel, Inconel, high-purity nickel, and niobium.

The equipment and experimental procedure for running stress-rupture and tensile tests in controlled environments have been described previously [2].
At 1500 and $1700^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ (816 and $927^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ) air or nitrogen decreased the creep rate, and CO and $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ had a strengthening effect.
At $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ ( $816^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ) the creep strength was essentially the same in argon and hydrogen but was less in oxygen than in argon.
Effect of Environment on the Creep Properties of Type 304 Stainless Steel at $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ (816°C) and 3400 psi.
Effect of Environment on the Creep Properties of Type 304 Stainless Steel at $1700^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ $(927^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ and 1200 psi.
At $1700^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ ( $927^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ) the creep life was less in hydrogen than in argon but was strengthened in an oxygen environment.
The reduction of strength observed at $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ ( $816^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ) in oxygen was somewhat unexpected.
In an effort to more fully evaluate the influence of oxygen on the creep properties of type 304 stainless steel, a series of creep tests were run in various argon-oxygen mixtures.
The oxygen-argon mixtures were prepared in standard gas cylinders, and a flow rate through the test chamber of approximately $0.1\mathrm{ft}^{3}/\mathrm{hr}$ was used.

section: Influence of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ on the Tensile Properties of Type 304 Stainless Steel
text: Because of the observed increase in carbon concentration as a result of exposure to $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ , a series of tensile tests were run in an effort to evaluate the influence of this carbon increase on the rupture ductility.

section: Influence of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ on the Tensile Properties of Type 304 Stainless Steel
text: A group of specimens 0.020 in. thick were exposed to flowing $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ for times up to 1000 hr and at temperatures ranging from 1100 to $1700^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ (593 to $927^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ).

section: Influence of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ on the Tensile Properties of Type 304 Stainless Steel
text: Control specimens were run in argon to separate the influences of thermal treatment and exposure to $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ .

section: Influence of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ on the Tensile Properties of Type 304 Stainless Steel
text: Pairs of specimens annealed under duplicate conditions were tested in tension at 75 and $1250^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ (25 and $677^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ); the results are summarized in Table III.

section: Influence of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ on the Tensile Properties of Type 304 Stainless Steel
text: The control specimens showed that subsequent heating in argon at less than the $1900^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ ( $1038^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ) homogenization temperature caused a change in the material properties.

section: Influence of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ on the Tensile Properties of Type 304 Stainless Steel
text: The tensile ductility of the controls at $1250^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ ( $677^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ) was increased by the thermal treatment, the magnitude of the improvement increasing with increasing annealing temperature.

section: Influence of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ on the Tensile Properties of Type 304 Stainless Steel
text: At both 75 and $1250^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ (25 and $677^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ) the tensile ductility was decreased by exposure to $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ .

For example, the rupture lives of two specimens tested in argon and in hydrogen at 10,000 psi were essentially the same, but differed by a factor of 3 between the pair of specimens tested at 3500 psi.

Another important characteristic of this effect is that the environmental influence was manifested through both an increase in the secondary creep rate and a decrease in the time required for tertiary creep to occur, as was obvious from the pair of tests at 3500 psi.

section: Effect of Carbon on the Room-Temperature Bend Properties of Niobium
text: The results of tests to evaluate the effects of nitrogen, oxygen, and hydrogen on the room-temperature bend properties of niobium have been described previously [3].

section: Effect of Carbon on the Room-Temperature Bend Properties of Niobium
text: Carbon concentrations as great as 2100 ppm did not seriously reduce the room-temperature ductility of niobium.

At $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ ( $816^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ) the creep rate was higher in $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ than in CO (Fig.
1), a behavior consistent with expectations since the carburization rate was higher in CO than in $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ .
At $1700^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ ( $927^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ) the creep-rupture behavior was similar to that just described.
Carbide distribution of specimens exposed to $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ was affected by stress (cf.
14 and 15) and by prestraining (cf.

This is illustrated quite well by the work of Garafalo [6] on the ability of carbides to retard the movement of dislocations in type 316 stainless steel during creep at 1100, 1300, and $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ (593, 704, and $816^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ).

Specimens were solution-annealed, prestrained, annealed at $900^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ ( $482^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ) to precipitate the carbides along dislocations, and creep-tested in the above temperature range.
of prestraining was found to be important since agglomeration occurred at prestrains in excess of $25\%$ .
If the carbide formed is $\mathrm{Cr}_{23}\mathrm{C}_6$ , 1.67 wt $\%$ Cr will be taken out of solution for every 0.1 wt $\%$ C added.

During tests in pure nitrogen nitrides were formed at 1500 and $1700^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ (816 and $927^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ) but those formed at $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ ( $816^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ) were only in areas that had undergone large deformation.
At $1700^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ ( $927^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ), nitride formation was more general and large quantities of nitrogen were introduced.

A series of tests at $1700^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ ( $927^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ) in air showed that the formation of nitrides was a definite function of the strain rate and probably depended also upon the time of exposure (Table II).
At $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ ( $816^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ) no nitride formation was observed in specimens tested for as long as 3025 hr in air.
However, Dulis and Smith [4] describe a type 304 stainless steel specimen tested in air at $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ ( $816^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ) for 10,000 hr in which nitrides were formed.

On the basis of the results just described it was concluded that the formation of nitrides in type 304 stainless steel tested in air was a function of the strain rate, the temperature, and the time of exposure.

The influence of oxygen on the creep properties was one of strengthening at $1700^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ ( $927^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ), the mechanism probably being that of internal oxidation, but was somewhat deleterious at $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ ( $816^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ) (cf.
In an effort to better understand the influence of oxygen at $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ ( $816^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ) a series of tests at various oxygen partial pressures were run.

However, they cause strengthening by the mechanism of internal oxidation in this material at $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ ( $816^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ) and oxygen pressures near 1 atm to be viewed with skepticism.

In light of the observations made regarding the behavior of materials in pure oxygen at $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ ( $816^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ), there is some doubt as to the mechanism responsible for the enhancement of creep properties observed in air at this temperature.
It was pointed out previously that no nitrides were visible in specimens tested in air at $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ ( $816^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ).
observed strengthening in air at $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ ( $816^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ) was due to nitrogen near the surface inhibiting the formation and propagation of cracks.

At $1700^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ ( $927^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ), however, oxidation did seem to provide some degree of strengthening, and the enhancement of creep properties in air may be a consequence of both nitriding and oxidation.

At $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ ( $816^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ) the creep properties were essentially the same in argon and hydrogen; at $1700^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ ( $927^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ), however, they were inferior in hydrogen, probably because of the removal of carbon by the hydrogen.
An improvement in the creep resistance of type 304 stainless steel was obtained by the addition of carbon but was accompanied by a reduction in rupture ductility.
It is conceivable that carbon concentrations in excess of 1 or 2 wt % may result over long periods of use in a carburizing environment.
16) showed that the strengthening effect of an oxidizing environment was manifested principally through increasing the time of tertiary creep.
19) that severe cracking occurred in an oxidizing atmosphere but that none of the cracks were able to propagate to failure.
Similar observations were made of "A" nickel and reported previously [7].
A significant observation made during the current work was that the creep behavior of Inconel is the same in air and in oxygen at $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ ( $816^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ).
Different rates were observed for type 304 stainless steel under similar conditions.
Since Inconel can be carburized and nitrided in a manner similar to type 304 stainless steel, the introduction of significant quantities of carbon and nitrogen results in strengthening.

Two obvious possibilities deserve attention: (1) some chemical change occurs; for example, subsurface oxides may be reduced by hydrogen to form water such as has been observed in copper [8], although this results in a loss of ductility, which is contrary to experimental observations in the present case; (2) carbon is removed by surface decarburization.
Another possibility, resulting from the mechanism proposed for low-temperature embrittlement [9], is that segregation of hydrogen around a crack nuclei is required for a crack to form.
For example, Livshitz et al. [10] observed that welds in steels were embrittled by prolonged heating in hydrogen in the range 500 to $600^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
The quite high mobility of hydrogen [11] in nickel at elevated temperatures,

Any review of the huge volume of work carried out in an effort to define and understand high-temperature creep processes is beyond the scope of this paper, and the reader is referred to the excellent review by Davies and Dennison [12].
However, the concept of high-temperature stress rupture, as presented by Cottrell [13], is worthy of brief mention because of its relevance.
strengthener, concentrations in excess of 3000 ppm result in serious embrittlement.
For example, increasing the nitrogen content from 48 ppm to 460 ppm changes the yield strength from 41,000 psi to 84,200 psi.
However, a concentration as high as 1000 ppm drastically reduces the rupture ductility.
Hydrogen concentrations in excess of 500 ppm seriously embrittle niobium at room temperature.
The absorption rate of hydrogen in niobium seems to be greatest in the range 500 to $600^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , where a stable hydride is formed.

A very limited number of creep and tensile tests at approximately $1800^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ (982°C) showed that moderate oxygen and nitrogen additions improved the high-temperature strength of niobium.
For example, increasing the carbon concentration from 140 to 2100 ppm increased the yield strength only from 41,000 to 47,400 psi.

The experimental observations which have been presented illustrate the effects of several gaseous environments on the mechanical properties of type 304 stainless steel, Inconel, high-purity nickel, and niobium.

Type 304 stainless steel was studied over the range 1500 to $1700^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ (816 to $927^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ) in environments of Ar, $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ , CO, $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ , $\mathrm{N}_{2}$ , air, and $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ .

To evaluate the influence of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ on rupture ductility, a series of tensile tests were run on samples which had been exposed to $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ for various times over the range 1100 to $1700^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ (593 to $927^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ).
Inconel was studied in Ar, $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ , $\mathrm{N}_{2}$ , $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ , and air environments at $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ (816°C).
High-purity nickel was studied in argon and hydrogen environments at 1500 and $1700^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ (816 and $927^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ).
Air and nitrogen increase the creep resistance at 1500 and $1700^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ (816 and $927^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ).
Embrittlement results from additions of nitrogen greater than 1000 ppm, of oxygen in excess of 3000 ppm, and of hydrogen greater than 500 ppm.
Carbon additions as high as 2100 ppm do not seriously reduce the ductility.
